author: dplyr_fan
content:
If you know SQL, you already know dplyr. The pipe %>% (or |>) just chains verbs the same way a query reads top to bottom.

filter() is WHERE. group_by() + summarise() is GROUP BY. select() is the column list. arrange() is ORDER BY.

The difference: SQL forces you to write them out of order (SELECT first, WHERE later). dplyr lets you write them in the order they actually run.
code:
flights %>%
  filter(month == 1) %>%
  group_by(carrier) %>%
  summarise(avg_delay = mean(dep_delay, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  arrange(desc(avg_delay))

comments.author: vectorize
comments.content: The out-of-order thing is exactly why SQL confuses beginners. dplyr reading like a sentence is the whole selling point.

comments.author: tidyverse
comments.content: Worth adding: na.rm = TRUE is the gotcha. SQL's AVG() skips NULLs automatically, but mean() in R returns NA if any value is missing.

author: tidyverse
content:
Coming from pandas? A data.frame is R's DataFrame, but the indexing is flipped from what you expect.

In pandas you reach for df.loc and df.iloc. In R you use df[rows, cols] with a comma. Leave one side blank to mean 'all of them'.

df[1, ] is the first row, df[, 1] is the first column. The comma is not optional.
code:
# rows where mpg > 20, all columns
mtcars[mtcars$mpg > 20, ]

# all rows, just the 'hp' column
mtcars[, "hp"]

comments.author: dplyr_fan
comments.content: The forgotten comma is the #1 error for pandas converts. df[1] in R grabs a column, not a row, which feels backwards at first.

author: vectorize
content:
Hot take: writing a for loop to transform a vector in R is almost always a mistake.

R is vectorized at its core. ifelse(), the math operators, most functions already work on the whole vector at once. The loop is slower and harder to read.

If you're looping in R, ask whether the operation already broadcasts. It usually does.
code:
# slow, un-R-like
out <- numeric(length(x))
for (i in seq_along(x)) out[i] <- x[i] * 2

# the R way
out <- x * 2

comments.author: tidyverse
comments.content: Same lesson as NumPy in Python. The moment you write a loop over array elements, there's usually a vectorized call hiding right there.

comments.author: dplyr_fan
comments.content: Caveat: when each step depends on the previous one (cumulative state), a loop or purrr::accumulate is fair. Vectorization isn't magic for sequential logic.

author: dplyr_fan
content:
Spot the bug. This R code is supposed to keep only the rows where region is 'West', but it returns weird half-empty results.

What went wrong?
code:
df[df$region = "West", ]

# vs what you actually meant:
df[df$region == "West", ]

comments.author: vectorize
comments.content: Single = is assignment, == is comparison. Same trap as SQL WHERE region = 'West' (one equals) vs R/Python comparison (two). Switching languages all day will get you.

comments.author: tidyverse
comments.content: In real code the single = would actually error here, but the deeper fix is filter(df, region == "West") so the intent is unmistakable.

author: tidyverse
content:
ELI5: what is a factor in R?

Imagine a dropdown menu with a fixed list of options: Small, Medium, Large. A factor is a column that can only hold values from that list, and R remembers the order.

It's like a SQL ENUM or pandas Categorical. Great for plotting and grouping, but it bites you when you forget a number got stored as a label.
code:
size <- factor(c("S", "L", "M", "S"),
               levels = c("S", "M", "L"))
levels(size)  # "S" "M" "L"
as.integer(size)  # 1 3 2 1

comments.author: vectorize
comments.content: The classic horror: as.numeric(factor) gives you the level codes, not the original numbers. Always do as.numeric(as.character(x)) for numeric factors.

author: vectorize
content:
TIL the pipe is now built into base R. You no longer need to library(magrittr) or even dplyr just to chain functions.

R 4.1+ ships the native |> pipe. It feeds the left side into the first argument of the right side, same idea as method chaining in pandas.

For most everyday work, |> and %>% are interchangeable.

comments.author: dplyr_fan
comments.content: One difference worth knowing: %>% lets you use a . placeholder anywhere, the native |> historically needed the value as the first arg. Newer R added _ placeholder but only with named args.

author: dplyr_fan
content:
Common R interview question: what's the difference between <- and =?

Both assign, but the convention is <- for assigning variables and = for passing named arguments inside a function call. Mixing them up rarely breaks code, but it signals you learned by copy-paste.

Good answer: mention that <- works anywhere, while = as assignment is restricted at the top level of a call.
code:
x <- 10            # variable assignment
mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)  # = is for named args

# this assigns AND works, but reads badly:
mean(x = 10)

comments.author: tidyverse
comments.content: Bonus points if you mention that mean(x = 10) creates the binding only inside the call, so x doesn't exist afterward. That trips people up.

author: vectorize
content:
Mental model: R's NA is not Python's None and not SQL's NULL, even though they feel similar.

NA is 'a value that exists but is unknown,' and it's contagious. Any arithmetic touching NA returns NA, just like NULL poisons a SQL expression.

That's why sum(x) gives NA if x has one missing value. Reach for na.rm = TRUE to tell R to skip them, the same way SUM() ignores NULLs in SQL.
code:
x <- c(2, 4, NA, 8)
sum(x)              # NA
sum(x, na.rm = TRUE)  # 14
is.na(x)            # FALSE FALSE TRUE FALSE

comments.author: dplyr_fan
comments.content: And never test NA with ==. x == NA returns NA, not TRUE. You must use is.na(x). Same reason SQL makes you write IS NULL instead of = NULL.

comments.author: tidyverse
comments.content: R even has typed NAs: NA_integer_, NA_real_, NA_character_. Mostly invisible until a function complains about type, then suddenly it matters.

author: tidyverse
content:
Reshaping data confuses everyone. Here's the long vs wide idea in one breath, mapped to tools you know.

Wide: one row per subject, a column per measurement. Long: one row per measurement. tidyr's pivot_longer() and pivot_wider() do the conversion, exactly like pandas melt() and pivot().

Rule of thumb: ggplot and dplyr want long data. Humans reading a table want wide. You'll pivot constantly between the two.
code:
# wide -> long, like pandas melt
long <- pivot_longer(df,
  cols = c(q1, q2, q3),
  names_to = "quarter",
  values_to = "sales")

comments.author: vectorize
comments.content: The mnemonic that stuck for me: 'tidy = long'. Each variable a column, each observation a row. Once data is long, every downstream step gets easier.

author: dplyr_fan
content:
If a dplyr summarise() is dropping rows you expected to keep, check whether you're still grouped.

group_by() sticks to the data frame until you remove it. summarise() peels off one grouping layer, but nested groups can linger and silently change later joins or mutates.

When in doubt, end your chain with ungroup(). It's the equivalent of resetting after a SQL GROUP BY before you run another aggregate.
code:
sales %>%
  group_by(region, month) %>%
  summarise(total = sum(amount), .groups = "drop")
# .groups = "drop" ungroups for you

comments.author: tidyverse
comments.content: The .groups = "drop" argument is the clean fix and it silences that 'summarise has grouped output' message everyone ignores until it bites them.

comments.author: vectorize
comments.content: Learned this the hard way when a downstream mutate() ran per-group instead of over the whole frame. Numbers looked plausible, which is the dangerous part.
